title: CardioRisk AI
subtitle: Cardiovascular Risk Prediction System
overview: An ensemble clinical-risk classifier combining XGBoost and Logistic Regression on a 920-patient UCI Heart Disease dataset. Deployed as a physician-facing Streamlit dashboard with real-time risk scoring and explainable AI breakdowns.
problem: Cardiovascular disease is the leading cause of death globally. Early risk identification is effective but depends on physician availability and consistent application of clinical criteria — both scarce in overburdened healthcare systems.
solution: An ML ensemble that ingests 13 clinical features (age, cholesterol, blood pressure, ECG results, etc.), produces a calibrated risk score with confidence intervals, and explains which features drove the prediction using SHAP values.
architecture: UCI dataset preprocessing → feature engineering → XGBoost + Logistic Regression ensemble → 5-fold cross-validation → SHAP explainability layer → Streamlit dashboard with real-time input and risk output
features: Real-time risk scoring from 13 clinical inputs
SHAP-based feature importance breakdown per prediction
Model diagnostics: ROC curve, confusion matrix, calibration plot
Physician-readable risk explanation in plain language
challenges: Class imbalance in the UCI dataset skewed naive models toward false negatives — dangerous in a clinical context. Addressed via stratified k-fold and threshold tuning to prioritise recall over precision. Calibration was critical: a model that outputs 0.9 risk should actually be right ~90% of the time.
results: AUC-ROC 0.905 validated via 5-fold cross-validation confirming generalisation across patient cohorts.
lessons: In clinical ML, a wrong prediction is not just an error — it has consequences. This project taught me to think about error types asymmetrically. False negatives (missed risk) are far more costly than false positives.
future: Extend to multi-disease risk scoring. Add longitudinal tracking so risk scores can be monitored over time rather than as one-off assessments.
